coredata.dc:title: Facile green synthesis of carbon dots from Pyrus pyrifolia fruit for assaying of Al3+ ion via chelation enhanced fluorescence mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Bhamore, Jigna R.
Jha, Sanjay
Singhal, Rakesh Kumar
Park, Tae Jung
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using Pyrus pyrifolia (pear) fruit via hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 6 h without using any further treatment or surface passivating agents. The synthesized CDs possess intense blue fluorescence under UV lamp at 365 nm and exhibit emission peak at 471 nm when excited at 390 nm. Because of specific surface functional groups, the synthesized CDs showed high selectivity to detect Al3+ ion through chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. The emission peak intensity of CDs at 471 nm was gradually enhanced with increasing concentration of Al3+ ion, thereby Al3+ ion was quantified by plotting calibration curve over the range of 0.005–50 μM with a detection limit of 0.0025 μM (2.5 nM). The nanoprobe was subsequently evaluated by assaying Al3+ ion from spiked water samples, signifying the utility of nanoprobe for assaying of Al3+ ion real samples. Furthermore, we also demonstrate that the bioimaging ability of CDs for imaging of Bacillus subtilis bacterial cell, suggesting that the nanoprobe can be used for cell imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent CDs
Pyrus pyrifolia fruit
Al3+ ion
Spectroscopic and microscopic techniques

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the CDs using pear fruit
Detection of Al3+ ion by CDs
Detection of Al3+ ion in water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financial support by Department of Science and Technology , Government of India ( EMR/2016/002621/IPC ). JRB acknowledges the fellowship of Ph.D. degree from the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Optical (absorption, excitation and emission) spectra of CDs derived from pear fruit. Inset picture shows the CDs under daylight (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right) and (b) Emission spectra of the CDs derived from pear fruit by exciting at different wavelengths from 350 to 500 nm.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of CDs derived from pear fruit, (b) HR-TEM image of CDs (Inset: histogram measured from HR-TEM image) and (c) life time data of CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs after addition of different metal ions (Hg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, K+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ and Al3+, 100 μM) (Inset: color change photograph of CDs upon the addition of above metal ions).

Metal ion-induced fluorescence enhance spectra of CDs with different concentrations of Al3+ ion ranging from 0.005–100 μM (Inset image shows the CDs under UV light after addition of 0.005–100 μM and calibration curve drawn in the range of 0.005–50 μM for the quantification of Al3+).
Fluorescence microscopic images of B. subtilis (a) bright field without CDs, (b) 405 nm (blue emission) and (c) 488 nm (green emission) with CDs as a fluorescent imaging probe.
Synthesis of fluorescent CDs from Pyrus pyrifolia (pear) fruit and their applications in Al3+ ion sensing and B. subtilis cell imaging.
Performance of the CDs derived from pear fruit as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Al3+ ion with the other reported methods.
Fluorescent CDs derived from pear fruit as a probe for assaying of Al3+ ion in spiked water samples.

head.ce:title: Facile green synthesis of carbon dots from Pyrus pyrifolia fruit for assaying of Al3+ ion via chelation enhanced fluorescence mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using Pyrus pyrifolia (pear) fruit via hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 6 h without using any further treatment or surface passivating agents. The synthesized CDs possess intense blue fluorescence under UV lamp at 365 nm and exhibit emission peak at 471 nm when excited at 390 nm. Because of specific surface functional groups, the synthesized CDs showed high selectivity to detect Al3+ ion through chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. The emission peak intensity of CDs at 471 nm was gradually enhanced with increasing concentration of Al3+ ion, thereby Al3+ ion was quantified by plotting calibration curve over the range of 0.005–50 μM with a detection limit of 0.0025 μM (2.5 nM). The nanoprobe was subsequently evaluated by assaying Al3+ ion from spiked water samples, signifying the utility of nanoprobe for assaying of Al3+ ion real samples. Furthermore, we also demonstrate that the bioimaging ability of CDs for imaging of Bacillus subtilis bacterial cell, suggesting that the nanoprobe can be used for cell imaging applications.

Schematic representation for fabrication of CDs using pear fruit and sensing of Al3+ ion via chelation enhanced fluorescence mechanism.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: In the earth crust, aluminium is one of the most abundant element, and its alloys are widely used as primary materials for manufacturing of various structural materials with potential applications in modern society [1]. It was noticed that the aluminium is known to be a biochemical enigma because of its non-functions in biochemical pathways. However, recent reports revealed that aluminium (Al3+) has shown high ability to interact with various biomolecules (cell cyto-structural neurofilaments, casein proteins, amyloid peptides, blood coagulation glycoproteins and thromobocytes) through non-specific fashion, yielding to disturb the biochemical pathways of various organs, which causes various disorder in the human body [2]. Even though its alloys and salts have shown remarkable applications in various fields, unfortunately the higher level of Al3+ ion causes various disease such as breast cancer, bone softing, Alzheimer's and Parkinson's dieses [3–5]. In this connection, World Health Organization (WHO) has set the maximum permissible limit of Al3+ ion is ~3–10 mg per day and 7 mg kg−1 per week (based on body weight) [6]. Therefore, the development of analytical method is essentially needed to monitor the concentration of Al3+ ion in water samples for safety health.

Currently, several analytical techniques such as atomic emission spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, voltammetry and high-performance liquid chromatography [7–12] have been demonstrated for the quantification of Al3+ ion in various samples. Though these techniques exhibit good sensitivity, unfortunately these methods often required sophisticated operation, and tedious sample preparation. Most recently, gold and silver nanoparticles-based colorimetric methods were developed for the quantitative measurement of Al3+ ion in water samples [13,14]. Though these strategies are simple and selective, but required specific and expensive organic ligands to modify the nanoparticles surfaces. Apart from these, several fluorescence-based analytical protocols have been explored for quantitative measurement of Al3+ in water samples. For example, Liu's group prepared naphthol aldehyde-tris derivate for fluorescence turn-on detection of Al3+ ion with limit of detection (LOD) of 1 × 10−7 M [15]. Gupta et al. developed a selective colorimetric and fluorescent chemosensor based on 1-(2-pyridylazo)-2-naphthol for the recognition of Al3+ ion [16]. Shoora and colleagues synthesized Schiff base receptors for fluorescence detection of Al3+ ion [17]. Fu et al. developed fluorescent turn-on method using electrochemically synthesized CDs for the quantification of Al3+ ion [18]. Up to now, few fluorescence-based “turn-on” mechanisms have been proposed for the detection of Al3+ ion such as photon electron transfer (PET) [19], fluorescence resonance energy transfer [20], metal ligand charge transfer [21] and chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) [18,22], respectively. Among these, PET and CHEF involve the change in the emission intensity with or without change in the spectral shift. However, expensive reagents and multiple steps are essentially required to fabricate the above organic receptors for Al3+ ion sensing, which may not suitable for the development of simple analytical strategy for assaying of Al3+ ion via fluorescence “turn-on” mechanism. Unquestionably, the development of facile and efficient green analytical methodology for assaying of Al3+ ion is still anticipated and significant interest.

Since the discovery of CDs in 2006, the CDs have emerged as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials with exciting and interesting applications including sensing, catalysis, drug delivery and bioimaging [23]. Due to their ultra-small size, surface passivation, unique optical and physico-chemical properties, the CDs have shown to be magnificent photo-luminescent nano-materials in various field of science [24–26]. In this connection, several synthetic routes such as arc discharge [27], laser ablation [28], electrochemical [29], thermolysis [30] and microwave [31] have been used for the fabrication of CDs and their applications in sensing, bioimaging and drug delivery. Fluorescent CDs have been used as probes for fluorescence detection of various metal ions. For example, Wee's group fabricated fluorescent CDs from bovine serum albumin and used as a probe for sensing of Pb2+ ion [32]. High quality CDs have been synthesized via carbonization using sweet pepper as a carbon source by Yin et al. for the detection of hypochlorite [33]. Huang's group demonstrated a simple and green synthetic route for fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for assaying of Hg2+ ion [34]. Wu et al. synthesized fluorescent CDs using Chinese medicine for the quantification of Hg2+ ion [35]. Apart from these, the fluorescent CDs have also been generated by using various sources as green precursors such as pasteurized milk [36], corn flour [37], water melon peel [38], jamun [39] and dried shrimp [40] for analytical and biomedical applications.

In this work, we fabricated fluorescent CDs from Pyrus pyrifolia (pear) fruit as a carbon source through hydrothermal heating at 180 °C for 6 h (Scheme 1 ). As a result, the fluorescent CDs were formed with quantum yield (QY) of 10.8%, demonstrating that the CDs from pear fruit exhibited higher QY than that of other CDs produced from various natural resources [36–40]. We demonstrated that the CDs derived from Pyrus pyrifolia fruit act as efficient electron donors for the selective chelation of Al3+ ion since pear fruit consists of various phytochemicals including soluble sugars (fructose, sorbitol, sucrose and glucose), organic acids (malic, citric and shikimic acids), triterpenoids (betulinic, oleanolic and ursolic acids), phenolics (phenolic acids, flavonols, flavones, flavan-3-ols, hydroquinones and anthocyanins) and sterols (cholesterol, campesterol, gramisterol, citrostadienol, β-sitosterol, ∆-stigmasterol and ∆5-avenasterol) [41]. Therefore, the surfaces of CDs can be fabricated with rich electron donating groups without use of any expensive, or toxic reagents, thereby offering an effective chelation between surface functional groups of CDs and Al3+ ion, which allows to develop highly selective and sensitive probe for sensing of Al3+ ion via fluorescence “turn-on” method through chelation enhanced fluorescence mechanism. To the best of our knowledge, the use of CDs derived from pear fruit is the first example for highly selective and sensitive Al3+ ion sensing via CHEF mechanism. Furthermore, the CDs act as a fluorescent probe for imaging of B. subtilis bacterial cells.

The pear fruits were obtained from the local fruit market, Surat, India. Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Cr(NO3)2·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Ba(NO3)2·4H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, AlCl3, Na2SO4·10H2O, KNO3, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All experiments were carried out using Milli-Q-purified water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CDs using pear fruit
ce:section.ce:para: The water-soluble CDs were generated by hydrothermal treatment using pear fruit as a carbon source. Firstly, 200 g of pear fruit was cut into pieces and grinded into mixture, and the obtained fruit juice was filter through the muslin cloth. To this, 200 mL of Milli-Q purified water was added and then the juice was transferred to 500 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave reactor and then reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 6 h. After the completion of the reaction, the obtained dark brown crude was filtered through the 0.45 μm syringe filter and the fluorescent CDs were collected by dialysis against deionized water using dialysis membrane-70 (molecular weight cut-off 12–14 kDa, HiMedia Laboratories Pvt., India) for 9 h at room temperature. The obtained CDs were kept at 4 °C for further experimental use.

To establish CDs-based analytical strategy for fluorescence detection of Al3+ ion, 2.0 mL of CDs solution was taken into a 5-mL glass vials and then 1.25 mL of different concentrations of Al3+ ion (0.005–100 μM) was added separately. The samples were vortexed for 1 min and kept stand for 10 min. The samples emission spectra were measured by fluorescence spectrometry for the construction of calibration curve for detection of Al3+ ion.

The absorption, emission and surface chemistry of synthesized CDs were measured by UV–visible (Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer, Ocean Optics, USA) and Cary Eclipse fluorescence (Agilent Technologies), and Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) (Shimadzu FT-IR 8400S, Japan) spectrophotometers. The size and morphology of CDs were investigated by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM) (JEOL 3010, Japan) instruments.

The fluorescence QY of the CDs was obtained by using standard reference sample (quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4) with 54% QY at 360 nm as emission wavelength.
Q CD
=
Q R
⋅
I CD
I R
⋅
A R
A CD
⋅
η CD 2
η R 2

where ‘Q’ stands for the QY, ‘I’ represents the integrated fluorescent intensity, ‘A’ stands for the absorbance and “η” is referred as the refractive index of the solvent; The subscripts ‘CDs’ represents carbon dots and ‘R’ stands for reference.

Pear (Pyrus pyrifolia) fruit was cheaply available from local fruit market and it consists of various phytochemicals including soluble sugars (fructose, sorbitol, sucrose and glucose), organic acids (malic, citric and shikimic acids), triterpenoids (betulinic, oleanolic and ursolic acids), phenolics (phenolic acids, flavonols, flavones, flavan-3-ols, hydroquinones and anthocyanins) and sterols (cholesterol, campesterol, gramisterol, citrostadienol, β-sitosterol, ∆-stigmasterol and ∆5-avenasterol) compounds [41]. In view of this, multi-functional organic groups can be effectively fabricated on the surfaces of CDs using pear fruit as a carbon source (Scheme 1). These phytochemicals of pear fruit play key role in generating efficient fluorescent CDs with high QY, which favors for the tuning of their sensing ability towards Al3+ ion due to the presence of rich electron donor groups on the surfaces of CDs. Before applying CDs as a fluorescent probe, we have investigated the effect of dialysis time for the production of CDs with high emission intensity (Supporting Information of Fig. S1). These results revealed that the emission peak intensity of CDs at 471 nm was increased with increasing dialysis time from 0 to 9 h, after that the emission intensity was decreased, suggesting that 9 h as the best dialysis time for the extraction of CDs with good yield.

To investigate the absorption and emission characteristics, and surface chemistry of CDs derived from pear fruit, spectroscopic techniques (UV–visible, fluorescence and FT-IR) were used for the spectral characterization of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1a, the intense absorption peak at 311 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of sp2 carbons in the CDs, while a shoulder peak at 338 nm was due to the n-π* transition of keto groups (CO) on the surfaces of CDs. This result suggests that UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs provides the information on electronic transitions of CDs derived from pear fruit. Furthermore, the CDs showed emission peak at 471 nm when excited at 390 nm and the emission peak underwent red-shift from 442 to 545 nm with increasing excitation wavelength from 350 to 500 nm (Fig. 1b), suggesting the reduction of band gap by incorporating hetero atoms in the nanostructured carbon materials, which favors for the generation of CDs with excitation-dependent behavior. The maximum emission peak intensity was observed at 471 nm under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The photograph in Fig. 1a represents that the CDs solution exhibit blue color emission under UV light irradiation at 365 nm, which is clearly visible to naked eye. The QY of the fluorescent CDs was found to be 10.8% by using quinine sulphate as a reference, which is much higher than the other reported methods [36,37].

Fig. 2a. illustrates the FT-IR spectrum of fluorescent CDs obtained from pear fruit. CDs showed broad peak at 3379 cm−1 confirms the existence of OH group stretching vibrations in the CDs, whereas CH stretching of alkyl group was observed at 2906 cm−1. The peak at 1751 cm−1 is corresponded to stretching vibrations of CO group and the stretching vibration of NH group is observed at 1610 cm−1. The peak at 1305 cm−1 is attributed to CH stretching vibrations of aromatic ring. The low intensity peak at 1043 cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO of CDs. The peaks at 922 and 835 cm−1 correspond to bending vibration of aromatic CH group. The morphological structures of the CDs were investigated by HR-TEM and DLS data (Fig. 2b and Supporting Information of Fig. S2). It is clearly observed that as synthesized CDs are spherical in shape with good dispersibility. The size of the CDs was 2.0 ± 1.0 nm, which is based on the histograms of the inset of Fig. 2b. The DLS data also confirmed that the average hydrodynamic radius of CDs is 4.0 ± 1.5 nm (Supporting Information of Fig. S2), which coincided with the HR-TEM histogram of CDs. The fluorescence lifetime spectrum of CDs reveals that the average lifetime of CDs is 4.89 ns (Fig. 2c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ ion by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To examine metal ion sensing ability of fluorescent CDs, 2.0 mL of CDs was taken into 5.0 mL glass vials and added separately 1.25 mL of 12 environmentally relevant metal ions (Hg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, K+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ and Al3+, 100 μM). Then, sensing ability of CDs was first assessed by digital camera and fluorescence spectrophotometry (Fig. 3 ). Among them, only Al3+ ion causes significant change in the intensity of blue color fluorescence and prominent fluorescence emission enhancement in the emission spectrum of CDs, suggesting the high selectivity of fluorescent CDs towards Al3+ ion, which allows to develop a simple analytical strategy for the detection of Al3+ ion via fluorescence “turn-on” mechanism. Before establishing analytical sensitivity, we have examined the effect of volume of 100 μM Al3+ ion from 0.25 to 2.0 mL on the emission peak of CDs (Supporting Information of Fig. S3). It can be noticed that the use of 1.25 mL of 100 μM Al3+ ion was significantly enhanced the emission intensity of CDs, revealing the formation of CDs-Al3+ ion complexes with high degree, which can cause to enhance the emission intensity of CDs. Therefore, we selected 1.25 mL of 100 μM Al3+ ion as the optimum volume for further experiments.

The sensing strategy of the fluorescent CDs for Al3+ ion is based on the formation of CDs-Al3+ ion complexes since the multiple organic groups (COOH, CHO, CO, and NH2) were present on the surfaces of CDs, which takes advantage for formation of Al3+ ion-selective CDs complexes. To assess this, exploratory efforts were illustrated that Al3+ acts as a “hard” acid and preferentially bind with “hard” donor groups (COOH, CHO, CO and -NH2) [42,43] facilitating to form effective Al3+ ion-CDs complexes, which results to enhance the fluorescence of CDs with high degree. To the best of our knowledge, the use of CDs derived from pear fruit as a fluorescent sensor is the first example for highly selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ ion via fluorescence “turn-on” (CHEF) mechanism.

To utilize the above advantage, the emission intensity of CDs was measured with the different concentrations of Al3+ ion (0.005–100 μM). As shown in Fig. 4 , the emission intensity of CDs at 471 nm approximately 77-fold (I/I0) increase in a manner that is proportional to the concentration of Al3+ ion, resulting to generate intense blue color emission under UV light irradiation, which could be distinguished with naked eye (Inset of Fig. 4). As a result, calibration curve was plotted by measuring the emission intensity values ((II0)/I0) versus the concentration of Al3+ ion (0.005–50 μM), where I and I0 represent the emission intensity at 471 nm in the presence and absence of Al3+ ion, respectively (Inset of Fig. 4). The calibration curve exhibits good linearity in the concentration of Al3+ ion (0.005–50 μM) with the correlation coefficient (R
2 = 0.9929). The detection limit of the probe for Al3+ ion was calculated as 2.5 nM (0.0025 μM) using 3 × standard deviation/slope of calibration curve (signal to noise ratio = 3). Table 1 shows the performance of the present method was evaluated by comparing with the reported methods for assay of Al3+ ion, signifying that the CDs-based fluorescent probe exhibited higher sensitivity as compare to other methods including membrane derivative device, chemosensor, fluorescent sensor and citrate-Au NPs [14,16,18,44–47]. These results demonstrated that the CDs-based fluorescent sensor offers the opportunity to detect Al3+ ion at nanomolar concentration without use of uncomplicated procedures or apparatus.

To demonstrate further the potential utility of CDs as a fluorescent sensor for Al3+ ion, different inorganic (metal ions - Hg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, K+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and Fe3+ and anions - Cl−, Br−, I−, F−, SO4 2−, S2−, Cr2O7 2− and PO4 3−, 100 μM and pesticides-isoproturon, chlorpropham, thiram, chlorpyrifos, dichlorovs and paraquate, 100 μM) influences on the sensing ability of CDs-based fluorescent probe for Al3+ ion were examined using the same optimal conditions (Supporting Information of Fig. S4). These results revealed that the CDs showed inability to interact with other chemical species and did not cause any significant changes in the emission intensity of CDs. Furthermore, the addition of mixture of various inorganic (metal ions and anions) and pesticides has significantly promoted the sensing of Al3+ ion, offering that the fluorescent probe is highly selective towards Al3+ ion even in the presence of various interfering chemical species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ ion in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the feasibility of the CDs derived from pear fruit for sensing of Al3+ ion, we applied this probe to detect Al3+ ion in water samples. The water samples were collected from different locations and treated as per the procedure [48]. Briefly, different concentrations (0.005, 0.05, 0.5 μM) of Al3+ were spiked on environmental water (tap, river and canal) samples and the Al3+ ion was quantified by the aforementioned procedure. As shown in Table 2 , the quantifications of Al3+ ion spiked water samples were observed in the range of 98.30–104.70%, with relative standard deviation (RSD %) <2.0%, offering a promising and reliable method for sensing of Al3+ ion with good accuracy and precision.

To explore the distinctive advantages of CDs derived from pear fruit, we used as probes for fluorescence imaging of B. subtilis bacterial cells. The bacteria culture and treatment have already well described in our previous reports [49,50]. The cultured B. subtilis bacterial cells were treated with CDs (10 μg/mL) and then fixed on glass slide using 4% paraformaldehyde solution in phosphate-buffered saline. As shown in Fig. 5 , the CDs-incubated B. subtilis cells exhibited blue and green fluorescence colors in fluorescence microscopy when excited at 405 and 488 nm, revealing that the CDs were easily and effectively internalized by B. subtilis bacterial cells, which suggests that the CDs could be distinctly located in cytoplasm, and especially cocooned in the nucleus of cells. Therefore, the CDs derived from pear fruit exhibit distinctive advantages such as ultra-small size, multi-color emission and multiple organic groups on the surfaces, enabling them to act as promising probes in sensing of Al3+ ion and in imaging of bacteria cells.

In summary, we developed a facile and economical method for the fabrication of fluorescent CDs using pear fruit as a carbon source. Due to the presence of various phytochemicals in pear fruit, the fluorescent CDs were fabricated with multiple organic groups on their surfaces, which facilitates to generate CDs with high quantum yield. Remarkably, the fluorescent intensity of CDs was significantly enhanced with addition of Al3+ ion, revealing the selective and strong interactions of rich electron donor groups with Al3+ ion, which facilitates to develop Al3+ ion sensor via fluorescence “turn-on” (CHEF) mechanism. Importantly, the CDs exhibit high selectivity to detect Al3+ ion at 2.5 nM, and show high selectivity towards Al3+ ion over other chemical species. Consequently, the CDs derived from pear fruit appear to be as a fluorescent probe for imaging of B. subtilis bacterial cells with blue and green color emissions. Therefore, we believe that the CDs derived from pear fruit can be used as a promising sensor for sensing of Al3+ ion and imaging of cells in the field of environmental and biological sciences.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financial support by Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (EMR/2016/002621/IPC). JRB acknowledges the fellowship of Ph.D. degree from the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2018.05.041.
